source.title: Scalable production and application of Pichia pastoris whole cell catalysts expressing human cytochrome P450 2C9

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: Scalable production of human CYP2C9 whole-cell catalysts in P. pastoris for preparative drug metabolite synthesis. Challenges: membrane protein expression, need for CPR co-expression, low activity/stability, and bioreactor reproducibility.

section_gists.heading: Abstract
section_gists.gist: Scalable bioprocess for co-expressing hCYP2C9 and hCPR in P. pastoris using bidirectional promoters. HTS identified best clones. Bioreactor optimization with glycerol-limiting and methanol co-feeding strategies improved production up to threefold. Preparative ibuprofen biotransformation demonstrated high selectivity for 3'-hydroxyibuprofen.
process_signals: HTS used to fine-tune co-expression
Bioprocess development based on rational optimization
Glycerol carbon-limiting at high μ gave highest production rates
Methanol co-addition with decreased μ gave best product-to-biomass yield and whole-cell activity
Up to threefold increases in production rates/yield over initial tests
Preparative-scale biotransformation validated catalyst performance
methods: Bidirectional expression systems with different regulation patterns
Bioreactor cultivations with glycerol-limiting and methanol co-feeding

section_gists.heading: Background
section_gists.gist: Human CYP2C9 is important for drug metabolism. Recombinant production is challenging due to membrane association, need for CPR, and low efficiency. P. pastoris is a promising host due to eukaryotic features and cost-effectiveness. Bidirectional promoters can tune co-expression. No prior study assessed bioprocess strategies for membrane-anchored CYP production in P. pastoris.
process_signals: hCYPs are membrane-associated, requiring membrane integration for activity
Co-expression of CPR is essential; host CPR often insufficient
P. pastoris chosen for its eukaryotic features and scalability
Bidirectional promoters allow tuning of CYP/CPR ratios
Previous work showed CYP2D6 activity in P. pastoris but no correlation between CYP levels and whole-cell activity
Goal: optimize whole-cell activity, not just CYP expression
failures_or_limits: Bacterial systems often fail to express active membrane proteins
Mammalian systems are slow and expensive
Purified enzymes have low efficiency and stability
Previous P. pastoris CYP2D6 work: highest CYP levels did not correlate with highest conversions

section_gists.heading: Strain generation
section_gists.gist: Seven bidirectional promoter combinations tested to co-express CYP2C9 and CPR. HTS using diclofenac and fluorometric assays identified PDC/PDF-CPR construct as best (53% conversion). Inverted orientation PDF/PDC-CPR also good (39%). Other constructs showed ≤20% conversion. Selected clone for bioreactor studies.
process_signals: Hypothesis: bidirectional promoters can fine-tune CYP/CPR ratio for optimal whole-cell activity
Promoters chosen: strong methanol-inducible (PDAS1, PDAS2) and derepressed (PPDC, PPDF) with different regulation patterns
Screening focused on whole-cell activity, not just CYP levels
Two HTS assays gave similar qualitative results
PDC/PDF-CPR construct gave highest activity
Inverted orientation also performed well, suggesting flexibility
Shortened promoter variants gave very low activity
methods: Gibson assembly for bidirectional promoter constructs
Transformation into P. pastoris MutS strain
HTS with diclofenac conversion (HPLC) and fluorometric MCF assay
failures_or_limits: Shortened promoter variants (DAS1-d6/DAS2-d8, etc.) gave only 1% conversion
CO-spectroscopy not used because CYP levels don't correlate with whole-cell activity

section_gists.heading: Production of P. pastoris CYP2C9/CPR whole cell biocatalyst in bioreactor cultures
section_gists.gist: First successful bioreactor cultivation. Previous unpublished attempts failed to reproduce shake flask activities. Comparison showed similar specific activities. pH optimization in chemostat: pH 5 gave threefold higher activity than pH 6 or 7. Subsequent fed-batches run at pH 5.
process_signals: Unexpected difficulty: earlier methanol-induced 5 L bioreactor cultures did not match shake flask activities
Current study confirmed bioreactor cells had similar diclofenac oxidation activity as shake flask cells
pH identified as critical parameter; chemostat at D=0.10 h⁻¹ used to isolate pH effect
pH 5 optimal, giving threefold higher specific activity
Decision: all further fed-batches at pH 5
failures_or_limits: Previous methanol-induced bioreactor runs failed to reproduce shake flask results (unpublished)

section_gists.heading: Biomass growth characterization in fed-batch cultures
section_gists.gist: Five fed-batch strategies tested: three methanol-free at μ=0.05, 0.10, 0.15 h⁻¹; two with methanol co-feeding at μ=0.05 h⁻¹ (pulses or constant 3 g/L). Biomass reached ~100 g/L DCW except FB1 stopped at 80 g/L due to heat. Methanol co-feeding did not alter growth curves. Recombinant expression did not limit growth. Physiology parameters (qGly, YX/Gly) were typical.
process_signals: Promoters PPDF/PPDC allow methanol-free or methanol-induced strategies
Methanol-free strategies avoid methanol toxicity and flammability
Methanol co-feeding required low μ (0.05 h⁻¹) to prevent MUT gene repression in MutS strain
FB1 (μ=0.15 h⁻¹) stopped early due to heat generation, but samples taken for analysis
Methanol co-feeding did not significantly affect biomass growth or glycerol uptake
Recombinant expression did not impair cell growth
methods: Carbon-limited exponential feeding for constant μ
Methanol pulses or constant 3 g/L via predictive-PI control
failures_or_limits: FB1 (μ=0.15 h⁻¹) temperature control failure at high biomass

section_gists.heading: Production of CYP2C9/CPR in P. pastoris whole cells in fed-batch cultures
section_gists.gist: Methanol-induced strategies (FB4, FB5) gave ~twofold higher final biocatalyst activity than methanol-free (FB1-3). In methanol-free, qp increased with μ (up to 2.5-fold at μ=0.15 vs 0.05 h⁻¹), but YP/X was similar (~11.5 AU/g). Methanol co-feeding increased YP/X to ~21.5 AU/g and qp to ~1.1 AU/g/h. Constant methanol (FB5) gave higher YP/MetOH than pulses (FB4). Low activity already at batch end due to derepression.
process_signals: Methanol-free: growth-coupled production, saturation at low μ, linear at high μ
Methanol-induced: exponential increase, twofold higher final activity
qp positively correlated with μ in methanol-free; YP/X unaffected
Methanol co-feeding increased both qp and YP/X
Constant methanol gave better YP/MetOH than pulses
Derepressed expression gave low but consistent activity at batch end, confirming promoter regulation
High reproducibility between runs
failures_or_limits: Methanol-free strategies gave lower final activity
Highest μ (0.15 h⁻¹) gave highest qp but lowest final activity due to early harvest

section_gists.heading: Preparative scale demonstration
section_gists.gist: 500 mL biotransformation of 2 mM ibuprofen using bioreactor-produced cells. 40% conversion in 86 h, mainly 3-OH-ibuprofen (35%) with high selectivity (M2/M1 ratio ~5). No 1-OH-ibuprofen detected. Space-time yield and specific production rate higher than previous fission yeast system. Oxygen, NADPH regeneration, and heme supply identified as potential improvement areas.
process_signals: Linear conversion up to 25% in 16 h, then slower to 40% at 86 h
High selectivity for 3-OH-ibuprofen, confirming CYP2C9 specificity
Performance superior to published S. pombe system (55% higher STY, 21% higher specific rate)
Limitations: dissolved oxygen, NADPH regeneration, heme group generation could further improve reaction
failures_or_limits: NADPH regeneration and heme supply not optimized

section_gists.heading: Conclusions
section_gists.gist: First scalable bioprocess for P. pastoris CYP2C9 whole-cell catalysts. Bidirectional promoter library enabled fine-tuned co-expression. Bioreactor optimization: high μ for qp in methanol-free; methanol co-feeding at low μ for YP/X. Preparative ibuprofen conversion achieved 40% with high selectivity. Model for other CYP catalysts and co-expression systems.
process_signals: Bidirectional promoter approach successful for fine-tuning
μ positively affects qp (growth-coupled production)
Methanol co-feeding maximizes YP/X and biocatalyst activity
Preparative scale validated scalability and selectivity
Framework applicable to other human CYPs and co-expression needs
failures_or_limits: Further improvements possible in biotransformation (oxygen, NADPH, heme)

section_gists.heading: Strain generation
section_gists.gist: Cloning: E. coli/P. pastoris shuttle vectors based on pPpT4. Seven bidirectional promoter constructs assembled via Gibson. Transformed into P. pastoris BSYBG11 MutS. HTS used to identify best clone due to clonal variability.
process_signals: Clonal variability necessitated HTS
Integration by homologous recombination

section_gists.heading: Fed-batch cultures
section_gists.gist: Carbon-limited exponential feeding for constant μ. Methanol co-feeding by pulses or constant 3 g/L using predictive-PI control with online methanol sensor. Off-line HPLC validation.
process_signals: Exponential feeding profile to maintain constant μ
Methanol control system with sensor and predictive-PI

section_gists.heading: Availability of data and materials
section_gists.gist: Data available from corresponding author on reasonable request.

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: Initial problem: need for scalable, selective human CYP catalysts for drug metabolite production.
Hypothesis: P. pastoris can be engineered for whole-cell CYP2C9 activity by co-expressing CPR using bidirectional promoters to fine-tune ratios.
Strain engineering: tested multiple promoter combinations; PDC/PDF-CPR gave best whole-cell activity in HTS.
Bioprocess challenge: earlier bioreactor attempts failed to match shake flask results; pH identified as key factor (pH 5 optimal).
Process optimization: compared methanol-free (growth-coupled, high μ for qp) vs. methanol-induced (low μ, high YP/X) strategies.
Methanol co-feeding boosted activity ~twofold; constant methanol better than pulses.
Preparative validation: successful ibuprofen conversion with high selectivity, outperforming previous systems.
Limitations: oxygen, NADPH, heme supply may limit biotransformation efficiency.
uncertainties_to_resolve: Why did earlier methanol-induced bioreactor runs fail to reproduce shake flask activities? (Not detailed)
What is the molecular basis for the optimal CYP/CPR ratio achieved with PDC/PDF promoters?
How does pH 5 enhance activity? (Mechanism not explored)
Can the biotransformation be further improved by addressing oxygen, NADPH, or heme limitations?
Is the process scalable beyond 5 L and 500 mL biotransformation?

events.summary: Scalable production of human CYP2C9 whole-cell catalysts is challenging due to membrane association, need for CPR co-expression, and low efficiency/stability.
Bidirectional promoters can fine-tune CYP/CPR co-expression ratios to optimize whole-cell activity.
High-throughput screening using diclofenac conversion and fluorometric assays identified PDC/PDF-CPR construct as best with 53% conversion.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers abstract, background, strain generation, and initial bioreactor results including pH optimization and fed-batch design.

events.summary: HTS identified construct 2C9-PDC/PDF-CPR as best with 53% diclofenac conversion.
Previous unpublished bioreactor attempts failed to reproduce shake flask activities.
Bioreactor cells showed similar diclofenac oxidation activity as shake flask cells.
Chemostat at D=0.10 h⁻¹ showed pH 5 gave threefold higher specific enzyme activity than pH 6 or 7.
Five fed-batch strategies designed: three methanol-free at μ=0.05, 0.10, 0.15 h⁻¹; two with methanol co-feeding at μ=0.05 h⁻¹.
Methanol-induced strategies gave ~twofold higher final biocatalyst activity than methanol-free.
Methanol co-feeding increased YP/X to ~21.5 AU/g and qp to ~1.1 AU/g/h; constant methanol gave better YP/MetOH than pulses.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers HTS identification of best clone, failure of previous bioreactor attempts, validation of bioreactor vs shake flask, pH optimization, design of fed-batch strategies, and analysis of production kinetics.

events.summary: Comparison of product-related parameters from fed-batch cultures shows methanol co-feeding improved qp up to 1.72-fold and YP/X up to 1.63-fold.
Methanol pulses-based process is preferable due to technical simplicity, as constant methanol control gave only ~20% higher YP/MetOH.
To maximize product activity and yield, prolong culture at low μ with methanol co-feeding; to maximize qp, use high μ glycerol-only fed-batch.
Methanol-free strategy at highest μ is recommended for maximizing qp and avoiding methanol drawbacks like high oxygen demand and heat.
Preparative biotransformation of 2 mM ibuprofen using bioreactor-produced cells achieved 40% conversion in 86 h with high selectivity for 3-OH-ibuprofen.
P. pastoris whole cell biocatalyst outperformed fission yeast system with 55% higher space-time yield and 21% higher specific production rate.
Biotransformation could be further improved by addressing dissolved oxygen, NADPH regeneration, and heme group generation.
μ positively affects qp, showing production is growth-coupled; high μ maximizes qp in methanol-free, while low μ with methanol maximizes YP/X.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains detailed analysis of fed-batch strategies, preparative biotransformation results, and conclusions linking process parameters to optimization criteria.

events.summary: Methanol concentration controlled at 3 g/L using predictive-PI control with online sensor.
Fluorometric HTS using MCF assay to quantify CYP activity via HFC fluorescence.
Bioreactor sample assays standardized to OD600=5 with 2 mM diclofenac for 20 h.
Preparative ibuprofen conversion: 500 mL, OD600=150, 206 mg ibuprofen, 86 h.
NMR used for product identification: 1H and 13C NMR spectra recorded.
methods_or_tools: Bruker Avance III 300 MHz FT NMR

events.summary: Data availability limited: datasets available only on reasonable request.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains detailed analytical methods and preparative scale protocol, but no new process evolution events beyond design/experiment descriptions. Relevance is medium as it provides methodological details supporting earlier process claims.
This chunk contains only reference list and publisher information, no research evolution events.

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Scalable production of human CYP2C9 whole-cell catalysts in P. pastoris for preparative drug metabolite synthesis, overcoming challenges of membrane protein expression, CPR co-expression, low activity/stability, and bioreactor reproducibility.

canonical_events.summary: Scalable production of human CYP2C9 whole-cell catalysts is challenging due to membrane association, need for CPR co-expression, and low efficiency/stability.
Bidirectional promoters can fine-tune CYP/CPR co-expression ratios to optimize whole-cell activity.
High-throughput screening using diclofenac conversion and fluorometric assays identified PDC/PDF-CPR construct as best with 53% conversion.
Previous unpublished bioreactor attempts failed to reproduce shake flask activities.
Bioreactor cells showed similar diclofenac oxidation activity as shake flask cells, confirming reproducibility.
Chemostat at D=0.10 h⁻¹ showed pH 5 gave threefold higher specific enzyme activity than pH 6 or 7.
Five fed-batch strategies designed: three methanol-free at μ=0.05, 0.10, 0.15 h⁻¹; two with methanol co-feeding at μ=0.05 h⁻¹ (pulses or constant 3 g/L).
Methanol-induced strategies gave ~twofold higher final biocatalyst activity than methanol-free; methanol co-feeding increased YP/X to ~21.5 AU/g and qp to ~1.1 AU/g/h.
μ positively affects qp, showing production is growth-coupled; high μ maximizes qp in methanol-free, while low μ with methanol maximizes YP/X.
To maximize product activity and yield, prolong culture at low μ with methanol co-feeding; to maximize qp, use high μ glycerol-only fed-batch.
Preparative biotransformation of 2 mM ibuprofen using bioreactor-produced cells achieved 40% conversion in 86 h with high selectivity for 3-OH-ibuprofen.
P. pastoris whole cell biocatalyst outperformed fission yeast system with 55% higher space-time yield and 21% higher specific production rate.
Biotransformation could be further improved by addressing dissolved oxygen, NADPH regeneration, and heme group generation.

edges.reason: Problem motivates hypothesis of using bidirectional promoters to tune co-expression.
Hypothesis tested by constructing promoter library and HTS to identify best clone.
Best clone selected, but previous bioreactor attempts failed to reproduce shake flask results.
Current study validates that bioreactor cells can match shake flask activity, resolving previous failure.
With reproducibility confirmed, pH optimization via chemostat identifies pH 5 as optimal.
Optimal pH used to design fed-batch strategies comparing methanol-free and methanol-induced conditions.
Fed-batch runs analyzed to compare biocatalyst activity and yields between strategies.
Analysis reveals growth-coupling and trade-offs between qp and YP/X.
Understanding of μ effects leads to design recommendations for maximizing different objectives.
Design recommendations applied to produce cells for preparative biotransformation.
Preparative results benchmarked against literature, showing superior performance.
Despite success, limitations identified for future improvement.

must_cover_points: HTS identification of best clone (PDC/PDF-CPR, 53% conversion)
Methanol-induced vs. methanol-free comparison (twofold activity increase)
Growth-coupling analysis (μ effect on qp and YP/X)
Design recommendations for maximizing qp or YP/X
Preparative ibuprofen biotransformation (40% conversion, high selectivity)
Benchmarking against fission yeast (55% higher STY, 21% higher specific rate)
